items.name: ConnectionsRx: A Distance-Based, Community Participation Intervention for Young Adults with Serious Mental Illness (SMI)
items.description: This project studies the efficacy of ConnectionsRx, a technology-based, distance intervention to increase community participation of young adults with serious mental illnesses. These young adults are receptive to technology-based interventions that target increased participation in the community. This population is already adept at using Short Message Servicing (SMS) and social media technologies and by harnessing these tools, providers can deliver non-invasive interventions that have the potential to reach participants in their communities, providing near-real time support. ConnectionsRx draws upon the Individualized Placements and Supports model and the Competence Enhancement model and consists of a 1-1 intervention with mobile supports and a peer-based social media support group. Specific objectives include: (1) conducting a randomized, controlled trial in order to (2) test the effectiveness, feasibility, and acceptability of ConnectionsRx. This project utilizes a randomized, controlled design with repeated measures (baseline, midpoint, and post treatment) with 80 young adults with serious mental illnesses, gathering data in three areas: efficacy (community participation, participation efficacy, and health-related outcomes), feasibility, and acceptability. Project objectives include: (1) gathering input from individuals with lived experience; (2) developing a ConnectionsRx training manual; and (3) evaluating the feasibility, efficacy, and acceptability of the ConnectionsRx intervention. Data were collected at three time points: at baseline and at three-month and six-month follow ups. The submitted data include the survey instrument along with the data for all three time points. Response Rates: N/A Presence of Common Scales: Temple University Community Participation (TUCP) Measures For a list of 27 different activities, The Temple University Community Participation (TUCP) measure asks individuals to indicate how many days in the past 30 they participated in the activity, whether this activity is important, and whether they feel like they do this activity enough, not enough or more than enough. The scale has good reliability and validity (Salzer et al., 2014; Salzer et al., 2015; Burns-Lynch et al., 2016). Several measures were created from the TUCP data: TUCP – Amount of Participation The TUCP asks about the amount of community participation over the past 30 days across 27 domains of everyday life. These domains span social, recreational, civic, and economic activities, such as going to restaurants, attending religious services, volunteering, or visiting friends and family. Sample items include: “In the past 30 days, how many days did you go to a restaurant or coffee shop?” and “How many days did you visit friends or family in their homes?” The total score is the sum of days across all activity types (range 0–810). Higher scores indicate greater amount of participation. TUCP – Importance of Participation The TUCP also assesses the number of activity domains individuals consider important in their lives, reflecting the value placed on community involvement across multiple areas. It provides insight into personal meaning and goal orientation, separating desired engagement from actual behavior. Respondents indicate whether each of the 27 TUCP activity types (e.g., volunteering, attending classes, exercising, socializing) is personally important. Sample items include “Is going to a park or recreation center important to you?” and “Is volunteering important to you?” The total score is the sum of activities marked “important” (range 0–27). Higher scores reflect a greater number of valued participation areas. This dimension enhances the TUCP’s conceptual strength by distinguishing between frequency and personal salience of activities. TUCP – Sufficiency of Participation The TUCP also measures sufficiency of participation - whether individuals feel they participate as much as they would like in valued activities. This construct captures alignment between desired and actual community engagement, providing a self-appraisal of fulfillment. The score is calculated as the percentage of activities deemed “important” for which the respondent reports “doing enough” (range 0–100). Higher percentages indicate greater perceived adequacy and satisfaction with participation. TUCP – Breadth Ratio of Participation The TUCP Breadth Ratio assesses the diversity of community participation by examining the number of important activity types engaged in at least once over the past 30 days. While the “amount” score reflects frequency, the “breadth” score highlights scope and variability, distinguishing between narrow and wide engagement across life domains. For example, someone who participates frequently in one or two areas (e.g., social visits and shopping) would have a lower breadth ratio than someone who participates occasionally across many areas. The measure is calculated as the number of important activities performed at least once divided by the total number of important activities, resulting in a score from 0 to 1. Higher scores indicate greater diversity and inclusion across community settings. Interpersonal Mattering Perceived mattering in close interpersonal relationships was assessed using the Interpersonal Mattering Short Form (IM-S; Moschella & Banyard, 2021). The IM-S is a 12-item self-report measure that captures the degree to which individuals feel that others in their social network notice them (Awareness), consider them significant and valued (Importance), and depend on them or look to them for support (Reliance). Participants rate each item on a Likert-type scale (typically 1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree), and subscale scores and a total mattering score are computed by averaging items, with higher scores indicating greater perceived mattering. Prior psychometric evaluations demonstrate a stable three-factor structure supported by confirmatory factor analysis, strong internal consistency for each subscale (Cronbach’s α values generally in the .86–.89 range), and good construct validity, including expected positive associations with well-being, social connectedness, and academic or interpersonal functioning. UCLA Loneliness Scale – 3-Item Version Loneliness was measured using the 3-item version of the UCLA Loneliness Scale (Hughes et al., 2004), a widely used brief screening tool designed to capture subjective feelings of social isolation. The scale includes three items reflecting core loneliness experiences: feeling a lack of companionship, feeling left out, and feeling isolated from others. Each item is rated on a 3-point scale (1 = hardly ever, 2 = some of the time, 3 = often), and scores are summed to create a total ranging from 3 to 9, with higher scores indicating greater loneliness. Despite its brevity, the 3-item scale demonstrates strong psychometric performance across diverse populations, including good internal consistency for a very short measure, strong correlations with the long-form UCLA Loneliness Scale, and good predictive validity for mental health outcomes, social support, and well-being. De Jong Gierveld Loneliness Scale Loneliness was also assessed with the short version of the De Jong Gierveld Loneliness Scale (De Jong Gierveld & Van Tilburg, 2006), a 6-item instrument designed to distinguish between emotional loneliness (feelings of missing an intimate attachment) and social loneliness (feelings of lacking a broader social network. Following standard scoring procedures, positively worded items are reverse-coded, and emotional and social loneliness subscale scores are computed by summing the number of loneliness-indicative responses, with higher scores reflecting greater loneliness. A total loneliness score can be derived by summing all six items. The short form has demonstrated solid psychometric properties across multiple countries and age groups, and strong convergent and discriminant validity relative to related measures of social isolation, quality of relationships, and mental health. Modified Differential Emotions Scale (mDES) Emotional experiences were assessed using a shortened version of the Modified Differential Emotions Scale (mDES; Fredrickson et al., 2013), which asked participants to report the greatest intensity with which they experienced several discrete emotions over the past 24 hours using a 0–4 scale (0 = not at all to 4 = extremely). The Positive Emotions subscale consisted of three items assessing feelings of love and closeness (“What is the most loved, close or trusted you feel?”), joy (“What is the most joyful, glad or happy you feel?”), and gratitude (“What is the most grateful, appreciative or thankful you feel?”). The Negative Emotions subscale consisted of three parallel items assessing anger (“What is the most angry, irritated or annoyed you feel?”), sadness (“What is the most sad, downhearted or unhappy you feel?”), and stress (“What is the most stressed, nervous or overwhelmed you feel?”). Subscale scores were computed by averaging the three items within each domain, with higher scores reflecting greater positive or negative emotional intensity. Bandura’s Self Efficacy Measure Self-efficacy for engaging in enjoyable activities was assessed using a 12-item scale adapted from Bandura’s self-efficacy framework. Participants rated each item from 0 to 100, indicating their degree of confidence in performing various activity-related behaviors, with higher numbers reflecting greater self-efficacy. The measure includes four theoretically derived subscales: Competence, Resilience, Social System Modification, and Empowerment, each represented by three items. Competence items assessed confidence in carrying out enjoyable activities (e.g., “How confident are you that you can participate in everyday, enjoyable activities at home?”; “How confident are you that you can plan enjoyable activities for yourself?”). Resilience items assessed confidence in using enjoyable activities to cope with challenges (e.g., “How confident are you that you can cope with stress using everyday, enjoyable activities at home?”; “How confident are you that you will find a way to participate in something you enjoy, even if there are challenges?”). Social System Modification items measured confidence in connecting with or mobilizing others through activity (e.g., “How confident are you that you can connect with others through everyday, enjoyable activities at home?”; “How confident are you that you will share your plans to participate in enjoyable activities with others who will encourage you to participate?”). Empowerment items assessed confidence in independently making choices and following through on activity participation (e.g., “How confident are you that you can make your own decisions about participating in everyday, enjoyable activities at home?”; “How confident are you that you will go to the enjoyable activities that you have planned?”). Each subscale score was computed by averaging the three constituent items, and a total self-efficacy score was calculated by averaging all 12 items, provided at least 10 responses were non-missing. Higher scores on all scales indicate greater perceived self-efficacy for engagement, coping, connection, and autonomous participation in enjoyable activities. PROMIS Global Health v. 1.2 Overall physical and mental health were assessed using the PROMIS Global Health v1.2 measure (https://www.healthmeasures.net/images/PROMIS/manuals/Scoring_Manual_Only/PROMIS_Global_Health_Scoring_Manual_30Aug2024.pdf, pp.7-11), a 10-item instrument that provides brief indicators of general health, quality of life, functioning, and recent symptoms. Items asked participants to rate their overall health, quality of life, physical functioning, mental health, satisfaction with social roles, and recent experiences of pain, fatigue, and emotional distress. Because the response scale used in this study was reversed from the original measure, all items were recoded so that higher values consistently reflected better health. Following PROMIS scoring conventions, four items were summed to create a Global Physical Health raw score, reflecting pain, physical functioning, physical health, and fatigue, and four separate items were summed to create a Global Mental Health raw score, reflecting quality of life, mental health, satisfaction with social activities, and frequency of emotional problems. Each raw score was then converted to a standardized T-score using the official PROMIS lookup tables, where T-scores have a population mean of 50 and standard deviation of 10, and higher values indicate better physical or mental health. Two additional items assessing General Health and Ability to Carry Out Usual Social Roles were scored individually but not included in the summary scores. The PROMIS Global Health v1.2 measure is widely validated and provides concise, reliable indicators of participants’ overall health status for use in behavioral and rehabilitation research. Self-Management Self-management related to daily and community participation was assessed using a 16-item scale developed for this study. Participants rated their agreement with each statement on a 1 to 6 scale, where 1 indicated strongly disagree and 6 indicated strongly agree. Items assessed an individual’s ability to set goals, maintain routines, problem-solve around barriers, sustain meaningful activity, and seek support when needed. Example items include “I know how to or have developed daily goals for myself,” “I can manage what I do on a day-to-day basis,” and “I can remove or overcome the barriers that are preventing me from doing what I want to do.” Several items reflecting difficulties with goals, routines, or problem solving were reverse coded so that higher scores consistently reflected better self-management (for example, “I find it difficult to develop long-term goals for myself” and “I struggle to create a routine that I follow on most days”). A total self-management score was created by averaging all items, provided that at least 13 of the 16 responses were non-missing. Higher scores on the resulting scale indicate greater self-management capacity. Precursors to Participation To assess the precursor activities that support community participation, we administered a set of questions grounded in a contingency-based model of behavior, which emphasizes that motivation, ease, and reinforcing consequences shape the likelihood of engaging in daily activities. Participants rated four attributes - ease, motivation, satisfaction, and enjoyment - across five common precursor activities that often precede participation: getting ready, doing something outside, contacting friends or family, planning activities, and traveling to a location. Each attribute for each activity was rated on a 1 to 4 scale (1 = not at all, 2 = slightly, 3 = very, 4 = extremely). Consistent with our analytic goals, responses were recoded to combine very and extremely into a single category, yielding a 0 to 2 metric (0 = no, 1 = slight, 2 = strong). For scoring, we averaged the five items within each construct to create indices reflecting the overall Ease of performing precursor activities, the Motivation to perform them, and a combined measure of satisfaction and enjoyment. The latter was conceptualized as Positive Automatic Reinforcement, representing the extent to which precursor activities are experienced as intrinsically rewarding. Higher scores on each index reflect greater ease, motivation, or positive reinforcement associated with engaging in the foundational steps that typically precede community participation. Capabilities Related to Participation Capabilities related to community participation were assessed using a 7-item scale developed for this study and grounded in the capability component of the COM-B model, which emphasizes the physical and psychological capacities required to engage in behavior. Participants rated each item from 1 (strongly disagree) to 6 (strongly agree). Items assessed whether individuals felt physically and mentally able to leave home, had the skills needed to locate and access activities, were comfortable navigating social situations, and could participate independently in the community. Example items include “I am physically and mentally well enough to leave my home on most days,” “I know how to find the locations and schedules for things I might want to do,” and “I do things in the community or go places in the community by myself.” Items reflecting difficulty or discomfort with participation were reverse coded so that higher values consistently represented greater capability (for example, “I have difficulty finding new activities or events that might be of interest to me” and “I do not feel comfortable talking to people I do not know in the community”). A capability index was computed by averaging the remaining items, with higher scores indicating greater capability related to participation. Opportunities Related to Participation Opportunities related to community participation were assessed using a 6-item scale developed for this study and grounded in the opportunity component of the COM-B model, which emphasizes the environmental and social conditions that enable or constrain behavior. Participants rated each item from 1 (strongly disagree) to 6 (strongly agree). Items assessed the availability, affordability, and accessibility of activities, as well as the presence of social supports that facilitate participation. Example items include “There are activities or events that are near me that I can reach by car or with public transportation,” “I get reminders about events or activities that I might be interested in,” and “I know people that will do activities with me.” Items reflecting barriers such as unaffordable activities, unaffordable transportation, or lack of encouragement were reverse coded so that higher scores consistently represented more opportunities (for example, “There are activities I want to go to, but I cannot afford them” and “I do not have people in my life that encourage me to do things”). An opportunities index was created by averaging the remaining items, with higher scores indicating greater environmental and social opportunities for community participation. Habits Related to Participation Habits related to activity participation were assessed using a modified version of the Habits Scale developed by Grove et al. (2014). The original measure assesses several domains of habitual behavior, and for the present study items were adapted to reflect habits surrounding daily and community participation. Items were rated from 1 (strongly disagree) to 6 (strongly agree). Four subscales were derived. The Negative Consequences subscale captures the emotional or physiological discomfort participants experience when they do not engage in activities, using items such as “If I do not engage in activities, I feel irritable,” “When I participate in activities, I feel better,” and “I feel anxious when I do not participate in activities.” The Patterned Action/Ease subscale reflects the degree to which activity participation follows stable, predictable patterns, including engaging in the same activities on most days, going to activities on the same days each week, participating for similar durations each time, or reliably frequenting the same places (e.g., store, park, friend’s home). The Strong Bonds subscale assesses the social influences that promote activity participation, such as being more likely to join an activity if someone else in the household is doing so, responding positively when invited by others, or being motivated by specific situations or opportunities to see people. The Automaticity subscale reflects the extent to which activity participation occurs with little conscious effort or planning, using items such as “I engage in activities that are important to me without conscious reminders” and “I tend to go out and do things without having to think about it or plan.” Subscale scores were computed by averaging the relevant items, with higher scores indicating stronger endorsement of each habitual pattern. CAINS Motivation and Pleasure Measure The CAINS Motivation and Pleasure scale includes three items that assess motivation for recreational activities and the experience or anticipation of pleasure. Item 1 measures current motivation to engage in hobbies and recreational activities. A score of 0 indicates no impairment, meaning the person is very motivated, initiates activities on their own, and persists well within normal limits, whereas a score of 4 reflects a severe deficit marked by no motivation at all and a near-total lack of initiation or persistence. Item 2 captures the frequency and variety of pleasurable recreational activities experienced in the past week. A score of 0 indicates at least three different types of pleasurable experiences occurring daily, while a score of 4 indicates no pleasurable experiences during the past week. Item 3 assesses anticipated pleasure in the upcoming week. A score of 0 reflects an expectation of many pleasurable experiences (seven or more), whereas a score of 4 reflects an expectation of no pleasurable activities at all. Convenience sampling Smallest Geographic Unit: N/A Participants were interviewed via Zoom by research assistant. Data were entered into Qualtrics by the research assistant.

items.name: ConnectionsRx: A Distance-Based, Community Participation Intervention for Young Adults with Serious Mental Illness (SMI)
items.description: This project studies the efficacy of ConnectionsRx, a technology-based, distance intervention to increase community participation of young adults with serious mental illnesses. These young adults are receptive to technology-based interventions that target increased participation in the community. This population is already adept at using Short Message Servicing (SMS) and social media technologies and by harnessing these tools, providers can deliver non-invasive interventions that have the potential to reach participants in their communities, providing near-real time support. ConnectionsRx draws upon the Individualized Placements and Supports model and the Competence Enhancement model and consists of a 1-1 intervention with mobile supports and a peer-based social media support group. Specific objectives include: (1) conducting a randomized, controlled trial in order to (2) test the effectiveness, feasibility, and acceptability of ConnectionsRx. This project utilizes a randomized, controlled design with repeated measures (baseline, midpoint, and post treatment) with 80 young adults with serious mental illnesses, gathering data in three areas: efficacy (community participation, participation efficacy, and health-related outcomes), feasibility, and acceptability. Project objectives include: (1) gathering input from individuals with lived experience; (2) developing a ConnectionsRx training manual; and (3) evaluating the feasibility, efficacy, and acceptability of the ConnectionsRx intervention. Data were collected at three time points: at baseline and at three-month and six-month follow ups. The submitted data include the survey instrument along with the data for all three time points. Response Rates: N/A Presence of Common Scales: Temple University Community Participation (TUCP) Measures For a list of 27 different activities, The Temple University Community Participation (TUCP) measure asks individuals to indicate how many days in the past 30 they participated in the activity, whether this activity is important, and whether they feel like they do this activity enough, not enough or more than enough. The scale has good reliability and validity (Salzer et al., 2014; Salzer et al., 2015; Burns-Lynch et al., 2016). Several measures were created from the TUCP data: TUCP – Amount of Participation The TUCP asks about the amount of community participation over the past 30 days across 27 domains of everyday life. These domains span social, recreational, civic, and economic activities, such as going to restaurants, attending religious services, volunteering, or visiting friends and family. Sample items include: “In the past 30 days, how many days did you go to a restaurant or coffee shop?” and “How many days did you visit friends or family in their homes?” The total score is the sum of days across all activity types (range 0–810). Higher scores indicate greater amount of participation. TUCP – Importance of Participation The TUCP also assesses the number of activity domains individuals consider important in their lives, reflecting the value placed on community involvement across multiple areas. It provides insight into personal meaning and goal orientation, separating desired engagement from actual behavior. Respondents indicate whether each of the 27 TUCP activity types (e.g., volunteering, attending classes, exercising, socializing) is personally important. Sample items include “Is going to a park or recreation center important to you?” and “Is volunteering important to you?” The total score is the sum of activities marked “important” (range 0–27). Higher scores reflect a greater number of valued participation areas. This dimension enhances the TUCP’s conceptual strength by distinguishing between frequency and personal salience of activities. TUCP – Sufficiency of Participation The TUCP also measures sufficiency of participation - whether individuals feel they participate as much as they would like in valued activities. This construct captures alignment between desired and actual community engagement, providing a self-appraisal of fulfillment. The score is calculated as the percentage of activities deemed “important” for which the respondent reports “doing enough” (range 0–100). Higher percentages indicate greater perceived adequacy and satisfaction with participation. TUCP – Breadth Ratio of Participation The TUCP Breadth Ratio assesses the diversity of community participation by examining the number of important activity types engaged in at least once over the past 30 days. While the “amount” score reflects frequency, the “breadth” score highlights scope and variability, distinguishing between narrow and wide engagement across life domains. For example, someone who participates frequently in one or two areas (e.g., social visits and shopping) would have a lower breadth ratio than someone who participates occasionally across many areas. The measure is calculated as the number of important activities performed at least once divided by the total number of important activities, resulting in a score from 0 to 1. Higher scores indicate greater diversity and inclusion across community settings. Interpersonal Mattering Perceived mattering in close interpersonal relationships was assessed using the Interpersonal Mattering Short Form (IM-S; Moschella & Banyard, 2021). The IM-S is a 12-item self-report measure that captures the degree to which individuals feel that others in their social network notice them (Awareness), consider them significant and valued (Importance), and depend on them or look to them for support (Reliance). Participants rate each item on a Likert-type scale (typically 1 = strongly disagree to 5 = strongly agree), and subscale scores and a total mattering score are computed by averaging items, with higher scores indicating greater perceived mattering. Prior psychometric evaluations demonstrate a stable three-factor structure supported by confirmatory factor analysis, strong internal consistency for each subscale (Cronbach’s α values generally in the .86–.89 range), and good construct validity, including expected positive associations with well-being, social connectedness, and academic or interpersonal functioning. UCLA Loneliness Scale – 3-Item Version Loneliness was measured using the 3-item version of the UCLA Loneliness Scale (Hughes et al., 2004), a widely used brief screening tool designed to capture subjective feelings of social isolation. The scale includes three items reflecting core loneliness experiences: feeling a lack of companionship, feeling left out, and feeling isolated from others. Each item is rated on a 3-point scale (1 = hardly ever, 2 = some of the time, 3 = often), and scores are summed to create a total ranging from 3 to 9, with higher scores indicating greater loneliness. Despite its brevity, the 3-item scale demonstrates strong psychometric performance across diverse populations, including good internal consistency for a very short measure, strong correlations with the long-form UCLA Loneliness Scale, and good predictive validity for mental health outcomes, social support, and well-being. A total loneliness score can be derived by summing all six items. items.name: Replication Code for: Tripping through Hoops: The Effect of Violating Compulsory Government Procedures
items.description: This deposit contains the code used to generate the analysis files and analyses used in the paper "Tripping through Hoops: The Effect of Violating Compulsory Government Procedures" All first appearances for traffic, misdemeanor, and municipal cases in the study site that were not resolved by two weeks prior to the court date for the time period between March 7, 2018 and July 15, 2019.

items.name: Code and Data for: Reducing Frictions in Healthcare Access: The ActionHealthNYC Experiment for Undocumented Immigrants
items.description: In 2016, New York City designed and implemented an intervention to reduce frictions in accessing safety-net care: randomly making initial primary care appointments for 2,428 undocumented immigrants. We leverage a novel survey-administrative data linkage to show that the program increased self-reported access to primary care, leading to 23% fewer emergency department (ED) visits. High-risk individuals' ED visits fell by 32% on average, driving the aggregate effect. Preventive care also increased among individuals visiting sponsored clinics.

items.name: Code and Data for: Reducing Frictions in Healthcare Access: The ActionHealthNYC Experiment for Undocumented Immigrants
items.description: In 2016, New York City designed and implemented an intervention to reduce frictions in accessing safety-net care: randomly making initial primary care appointments for 2,428 undocumented immigrants. We leverage a novel survey-administrative data linkage to show that the program increased self-reported access to primary care, leading to 23% fewer emergency department (ED) visits. High-risk individuals' ED visits fell by 32% on average, driving the aggregate effect. Preventive care also increased among individuals visiting sponsored clinics.
items.citationHtml: Sabety, Adrienne; Gruber, Jonathan; Bae, Jin Yung; Sood, Rishi, 2026, "Code and Data for: Reducing Frictions in Healthcare Access: The ActionHealthNYC Experiment for Undocumented Immigrants", https://doi.org/10.3886/E177861V1, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Selection on Welfare Gains: Experimental Evidence from Electricity Plan Choice
items.description: We study a problem in which policymakers need to screen self-selected individuals by unobserved heterogeneity in social welfare gains from a policy intervention. In our framework, the marginal treatment effects and marginal treatment responses arise as key statistics to characterize social welfare. We apply this framework to a randomized field experiment on electricity plan choice. Consumers were offered welfare-improving dynamic pricing with randomly assigned take-up incentives. We find that price-elastic consumers—who generate larger welfare gains—are more likely to self-select. Our counterfactual simulations quantify the optimal take-up incentives that exploit observed and unobserved heterogeneity in selection and welfare gains.
items.citationHtml: Ito, Koichiro; Ida, Takanori; Tanaka, Makoto, 2026, "Data and Code for: Selection on Welfare Gains: Experimental Evidence from Electricity Plan Choice", https://doi.org/10.3886/E190381, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Take-up of Social Benefits: Experimental Evidence from France
items.description: We report on two nationwide experiments with job seekers in France. We first show that a meeting with social services to assess eligibility and help with application to social benefits increased new benefit take-up by 31%. By contrast, an online simulator that gave personalized information on benefit eligibility did not increase take-up. Marginal treatment effects show that individuals who benefit the most from the meetings are the least likely to attend. Overall, without ruling out information frictions, our results suggest that transaction costs represent the main obstacle to applying for benefits or accessing government's assistance to help apply. Stratified random sampling. Registered job-seekers in Frnace .
items.citationHtml: Castell, Laura; Gurgand, Marc; Imbert, Clément; Tochev, Todor, 2026, "Data and Code for: Take-up of Social Benefits: Experimental Evidence from France", https://doi.org/10.3886/E209001, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: "SMEs and Workers During Crises: Evidence From the Covid-19 Pandemic in Uganda"
items.description: We use a five-year panel of Ugandan SMEs, supplemented with phone-survey data from August 2020, to analyze how the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic affected profits and employment. Most firms had employees, enabling us to investigate whether—and how—the crisis reshaped SMEs’ job-creation capacity, with particular focus on gender differences. Profits fell substantially for all firms, yet male entrepreneurs paradoxically expanded their workforce—suggesting that hiring under crisis may arise partly from social obligations. Meanwhile, female entrepreneurs bore heavier caregiving loads and relied more on extended family support, potentially hampering future growth through added caregiving and reciprocal obligations.
items.citationHtml: Gulesci, Selim; Loiacono, Francesco; Madestam, Andreas; Stryjan, Miri, 2026, "Data and Code for: "SMEs and Workers During Crises: Evidence From the Covid-19 Pandemic in Uganda"", https://doi.org/10.3886/E228103, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: A Signal to End Child Marriage: Theory and Experimental Evidence from Bangladesh
items.description: Child marriage remains common even where female schooling and employment opportunities have grown. We experimentally evaluate a financial incentive to delay marriage alongside a girls' empowerment program in Bangladesh. While girls eligible for two years of incentive are 19% less likely to marry underage, the empowerment program failed to decrease adolescent marriage. We show that these results are consistent with a signaling model in which bride type is imperfectly observed but preferred types (socially conservative girls) have lower returns to delaying marriage. Consistent with our theoretical prediction, we observe substantial spillovers of the incentive on untreated non-preferred types.
items.citationHtml: Buchmann, Nina; Field, Erica; Glennerster, Rachel; Nazneen, Shahana; Wang, Xiao Yu, 2026, "Data and Code for: A Signal to End Child Marriage: Theory and Experimental Evidence from Bangladesh", https://doi.org/10.3886/E192114V1-162561, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: The Consumer Welfare Effects of Online Ads: Evidence from a 9-Year Experiment
items.description: Research on the effects of online advertising on consumer welfare is limited due to challenges in running large-scale field experiments. We analyze a long-running field experiment on Facebook in which a random subset of users received no ads in their newsfeeds. Using an incentive - compatible deactivation experiment, we find no significant differences in users’ valuation of Facebook across a representative sample of 53,083 Facebook users in the ads and no ads groups. Our sample size allows for precise estimates, suggesting that either the disutility of ads is relatively small or that there are offsetting benefits, such as product discovery.
items.citationHtml: Brynjolfsson, Erik; Collis, Avinash; Deisenroth, Daniel; Garro, Haritz; Kutzman, Daley; Liaqat, Asad; Wernerfelt, Nils, 2026, "Data and Code for: The Consumer Welfare Effects of Online Ads: Evidence from a 9-Year Experiment", https://doi.org/10.3886/E225641V1, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Code for: Electronic Food Vouchers: Evidence from an At-Scale Experiment in Indonesia
items.description: We compare how in-kind food assistance and an electronic voucher-based program affect the delivery of aid in practice. The Government of Indonesia randomized across 105 districts the transition from in-kind rice to approximately equivalent electronic vouchers redeemable for rice and eggs at a network of private agents. Targeted households received 46 percent more assistance in voucher areas. For the bottom 15 percent of households at baseline, poverty fell 20 percent. Voucher recipients received higher-quality rice, and increased consumption of eggs. The results suggest moving from a manual in-kind to electronic voucher-based program reduced poverty through increased adherence to program design.
items.citationHtml: Banerjee, Abhijit; Hanna, Rema; Olken, Benjamin; Satriawan, Elan; Sumarto, Sudarno, 2026, "Code for: Electronic Food Vouchers: Evidence from an At-Scale Experiment in Indonesia", https://doi.org/10.3886/E167262, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: "Data and Code for: Convincing the Mummy-ji: Improving Mother-in-Law Approval of Family Planning in India"
items.description: Mothers-in-law, especially those in South Asia, can exert significant influence over women, often even more so than women’s husbands or other household members. Using data from rural India, we first show that mothers-in-law are more likely than husbands to a) disapprove of women’s family planning use; and b) want women to have more children, particularly sons, than what women themselves want. Next, using a field experiment, we show that providing women with vouchers for subsidized family planning services not only enabled them to initiate discussions about family planning with their mothers-in-law, but also increased their mothers-in-law’s approval of family planning.
items.citationHtml: Anukriti, S.; Herrera-Almanza, Catalina; Karra, Mahesh; Valdebenito, Rocio, 2026, ""Data and Code for: Convincing the Mummy-ji: Improving Mother-in-Law Approval of Family Planning in India"", https://doi.org/10.3886/E169141, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Replication data for: Household Matters: Revisiting the Returns to Capital among Female Microentrepreneurs
items.description: Multiple field experiments report positive financial returns to capital shocks for male and not female microentrepreneurs. But these analyses overlook the fact that female entrepreneurs often reside with male entrepreneurs. Using data from experiments in India, Sri Lanka, and Ghana, we show that the observed gender gap in microenterprise responses does not reflect lower returns on investment, when measured at the household level. Instead, the absence of a profit response among female-run enterprises reflects the fact that women's capital is typically invested into their husband's enterprise. We cannot reject equivalence of household-level income gains for male and female capital shock recipients.
items.citationHtml: Bernhardt, Arielle; Field, Erica; Pande, Rohini; Rigol, Natalia, 2026, "Replication data for: Household Matters: Revisiting the Returns to Capital among Female Microentrepreneurs", https://doi.org/10.3886/E116326V1-134871, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research
items.citation: Bernhardt, Arielle; Field, Erica; Pande, Rohini; Rigol, Natalia, 2026, "Replication data for: Household Matters: Revisiting the Returns to Capital among Female Microentrepreneurs", https://doi.org/10.3886/E116326V1-134871, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: The Marginal Disutility from Corruption in Social Programs: Evidence from Program Administrators and Beneficiaries
items.description: Concerns about fraud in welfare programs are common arguments worldwide against such programs. We conducted a survey experiment with over 28,000 welfare program administrators and over 19,000 beneficiaries in Indonesia to elicit the ‘marginal disutility from corruption,’ i.e., the trade-off between more generous social assistance and losses due to corruption and fraud. Merely mentioning corruption reduced perceived program success, equivalent to distributing more than 26 percent less aid. However, respondents were not sensitive to the amount of corruption—respondents were willing to trade off $2 of additional losses for an additional $1 distributed to beneficiaries. Program administrators and beneficiaries had similar assessments.
items.citationHtml: Hanna, Rema; Olken, Benjamin A.; Gaduh, Arya, 2026, "Data and Code for: The Marginal Disutility from Corruption in Social Programs: Evidence from Program Administrators and Beneficiaries", https://doi.org/10.3886/E192290, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research
items.citation: Hanna, Rema; Olken, Benjamin A.; Gaduh, Arya, 2026, "Data and Code for: The Marginal Disutility from Corruption in Social Programs: Evidence from Program Administrators and Beneficiaries", https://doi.org/10.3886/E192290, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Comparative Advantage of Humans vs AI in the Long Tail
items.description: Abstract: Machine learning algorithms now exceed human performance on a number of predictive tasks, generating concerns about widespread job displacement. However, supervised learning approaches rely on large amounts of high-quality labeled data and are designed for specific predictive tasks. Thus, humans may be required for a large number of tasks each of which are not commonly encountered -- the long tail -- because humans can make predictions for a broader range of outcomes and with exposure to much less data. We show that a self-supervised algorithm for chest X-rays, which does not require specifically annotated disease labels, closes this gap even in the long tail of diseases.
items.citationHtml: Agarwal, Nikhil; Huang, Ray; Moehring, Alex; Rajpurkar, Pranav; Salz, Tobias; Yu, Feiyang, 2026, "Data and Code for: Comparative Advantage of Humans vs AI in the Long Tail", https://doi.org/10.3886/E202185, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: My Professor Cares: Experimental Evidence on the Role of Faculty Engagement
items.description: We provide experimental evidence on the impact of specific faculty behaviors aimed at increasing student success for college students from historically underrepresented groups. The intervention was developed after conducting in-person focus groups and a pilot experiment. We find significant positive treatment effects across a multitude of short and longer-run outcomes. Specifically, underrepresented students in the treatment report more positive perceptions of the professor and earned higher course grades. These positive effects persisted over the next several years, with students in the treatment more likely to persist in college, resulting in increased credit accumulation and degree completion. Presence of Common Scales: A likert-type scale was used in surveys Random sampling
items.citationHtml: Carrell, Scott; Kurlaender, Michal, 2026, "Data and Code for: My Professor Cares: Experimental Evidence on the Role of Faculty Engagement", https://doi.org/10.3886/E169341, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Selection on Welfare Gains: Experimental Evidence from Electricity Plan Choice
items.description: We study a problem in which policymakers need to screen self-selected individuals by unobserved heterogeneity in social welfare gains from a policy intervention. In our framework, the marginal treatment effects and marginal treatment responses arise as key statistics to characterize social welfare. We apply this framework to a randomized field experiment on electricity plan choice. Consumers were offered welfare-improving dynamic pricing with randomly assigned take-up incentives. We find that price-elastic consumers—who generate larger welfare gains—are more likely to self-select. Our counterfactual simulations quantify the optimal take-up incentives that exploit observed and unobserved heterogeneity in selection and welfare gains.
items.citationHtml: Ito, Koichiro; Ida, Takanori; Tanaka, Makoto, 2026, "Data and Code for: Selection on Welfare Gains: Experimental Evidence from Electricity Plan Choice", https://doi.org/10.3886/E190381V1-142841, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research
items.citation: Ito, Koichiro; Ida, Takanori; Tanaka, Makoto, 2026, "Data and Code for: Selection on Welfare Gains: Experimental Evidence from Electricity Plan Choice", https://doi.org/10.3886/E190381V1-142841, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Code for: The (Lack of) Anticipatory Effects of the Social Safety Net on Human Capital Investment
items.description: How does the expectation that a child will receive government benefits in adulthood affect parental investments in the child’s human capital? Most parents whose children receive Supplemental Security Income (SSI) benefits overestimate the likelihood that their child will receive SSI benefits in adulthood. We present randomly-selected families with the predicted likelihood that their child will receive SSI benefits in adulthood. Reducing parents’ expectations that children will receive benefits in adulthood does not increase investments in children’s human capital. This zero effect is precisely estimated. Likely explanations include parents working more themselves, non-financial goals influencing investment, and families facing investment constraints. Random subset of households with a child receiving SSI.
items.citationHtml: Deshpande, Manasi; Dizon-Ross, Rebecca, 2026, "Code for: The (Lack of) Anticipatory Effects of the Social Safety Net on Human Capital Investment", https://doi.org/10.3886/E192865, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: A Signal to End Child Marriage: Theory and Experimental Evidence from Bangladesh
items.description: Child marriage remains common even where female schooling and employment opportunities have grown. We experimentally evaluate a financial incentive to delay marriage alongside a girls' empowerment program in Bangladesh. While girls eligible for two years of incentive are 19% less likely to marry underage, the empowerment program failed to decrease adolescent marriage. We show that these results are consistent with a signaling model in which bride type is imperfectly observed but preferred types (socially conservative girls) have lower returns to delaying marriage. Consistent with our theoretical prediction, we observe substantial spillovers of the incentive on untreated non-preferred types.
items.citationHtml: Buchmann, Nina; Field, Erica; Glennerster, Rachel; Nazneen, Shahana; Wang, Xiao Yu, 2026, "Data and Code for: A Signal to End Child Marriage: Theory and Experimental Evidence from Bangladesh", https://doi.org/10.3886/E192114, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Code for: "Effects of Enhanced Legal Aid in Child Welfare: Evidence from a Randomized Trial of Mi Abogado"
items.description: Children spend years in foster care, and there are concerns that bureaucratic hurdles contribute to unnecessarily long stays. Mi Abogado (My Lawyer) was introduced in Chile to enhance legal aid for foster children to accelerate family reunification. In a novel approach to policy making, the Chilean government randomized the introduction of the program for children living in institutions to evaluate effects on child wellbeing. Using registry data, we find that exposure to the program significantly reduced the duration of foster care without increasing subsequent maltreatment and placements, leading to substantial cost savings. The exposure also decreased criminal justice involvement and improved school attendance. The results show that investing in improvements to foster care services can improve child wellbeing in a cost-effective way.
items.citationHtml: Cooper, Ryan; Doyle, Joseph; Hojman, Andres, 2026, "Code for: "Effects of Enhanced Legal Aid in Child Welfare: Evidence from a Randomized Trial of Mi Abogado"", https://doi.org/10.3886/E219241V1, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: “The Challenges of Universal Health Insurance in Developing Countries: Experimental Evidence from Indonesia’s National Health Insurance”
items.description: To investigate barriers to universal health insurance in developing countries, we designed a randomized experiment involving about 6,000 households in Indonesia who are subject to a government health insurance program with a weakly enforced mandate. Time-limited subsidies increased enrollment and attracted lower-cost enrollees, in part by reducing the strategic timing of enrollment to correspond with health needs. Registration assistance also increased enrollment, but increased attempted enrollment much more, as over half of households who attempted to enroll did not successfully do so. These findings underscore how weak administrative capacity can create important challenges in developing countries for achieving widespread coverage. Non-poor informal households without health insurance.
items.citationHtml: Banerjee, Abhijit; Finkelstein, Amy; Hanna, Rema; Olken, Benjamin A.; Ornaghi, Arianna; Sumarto, Sudarno, 2026, "Data and Code for: “The Challenges of Universal Health Insurance in Developing Countries: Experimental Evidence from Indonesia’s National Health Insurance”", https://doi.org/10.3886/E138701V1-102300, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Technology, Taxation, and Corruption: Evidence from the Introduction of Electronic Tax Filing
items.description: This is data and code for the paper "Technology, Taxation, and Corruption: Evidence from the Introduction of Electronic Tax Filing" Many e-government initiatives introduce technology to improve efficiency and avoid potential human bias. Using experimental variation, we examine the impact of electronic tax filing (to replace in-person submission to tax officials) using data from Tajikistan firms. E-filing reduces the time firms spend on taxes by 40 percent. Further, among firms previously more likely to evade, e-filing doubles taxes paid. Conversely, evidence suggests that e-filing reduces tax payments among firms previously less likely to evade. These firms also pay fewer bribes as e-filing reduces extortion opportunities. These patterns are consistent with differential treatment of firms by tax officials prior to e-filing. Small and medium firms in Dushanbe that were not registered for e-filing in 2014.
items.citationHtml: Okunogbe, Oyebola; Pouliquen, Victor, 2026, "Data and Code for: Technology, Taxation, and Corruption: Evidence from the Introduction of Electronic Tax Filing", https://doi.org/10.3886/E131281, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Market Access and Quality Upgrading: Evidence from Four Experiments
items.description: Smallholder farming in many developing countries is characterized by low productivity and low quality output. Low quality limits the price farmers can command and their potential income. We conduct a series of experiments among maize farmers in Uganda to shed light on the barriers to quality upgrading and to study its potential. First, we document that quality is low but partly observable. Second, we show that the causal return to quality is zero, suggesting that the market for quality maize is effectively missing. Third, we generate experimental variation in access to a market for premium quality maize, combined with training on agricultural best-practices, and document large increases in both farm productivity and income. Fourth, we show that agricultural training alone does not affect agricultural outcomes. Our findings reveal the importance of demand-side constraints in limiting rural income and productivity growth. Smallholder households in Uganda.
items.citationHtml: Bold, Tessa; Ghisolfi, Selene; Nsonzi, Frances; Svensson, Jakob, 2026, "Data and Code for: Market Access and Quality Upgrading: Evidence from Four Experiments", https://doi.org/10.3886/E158401, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Public Information is an Incentive for Politicians
items.description: Two years prior to elections, two-thirds of Delhi municipal councilors learned that they had been randomly chosen for a pre-election newspaper report card. Treated councilors in high-slum areas increased pro-poor spending, relative both to control counterparts and treated counterparts from low-slum areas. Treated incumbents ineligible to rerun in home wards because of randomly assigned gender quotas were substantially likelier to run elsewhere only if their report card showed a strong pro-poor spending record. Parties also benefited electorally from councilors’ high pro-poor spending. In contrast, in a cross-cut experiment, councilors did not react to actionable information that was not publicly disclosed. Poor households in New Delhi in 2010. Municipal Corporation of Delhi councilors in the 2007–2012 term. Smallest Geographic Unit: Wards of the Municipal Corporation of Delhi
items.citationHtml: Banerjee, Abhijit; Enevoldsen, Nils; Pande, Rohini; Walton, Michael, 2026, "Data and Code for: Public Information is an Incentive for Politicians", https://doi.org/10.3886/E183581, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: All Eyes on Them: A Field Experiment on Citizen Oversight and Electoral Integrity
items.description: Can Information and Communication Technologies help citizens monitor their elections? We analyze a large-scale field experiment designed to answer this question in Colombia. We leveraged Facebook advertisements sent to over 4 million potential voters to encourage citizen reporting of electoral irregularities. We also cross-randomized whether candidates were informed about the campaign in a subset of municipalities. Total reports, and evidence-backed ones, experienced a large increase. Across a wide array of measures, electoral irregularities decreased. Finally, the reporting campaign reduced the vote share of candidates dependent on irregularities. This light-touch intervention is more cost-effective than monitoring efforts traditionally used by policymakers. Study group intervention: 698 municipalities in Colombia. Surveys: adult Facebook users (aged 18 years or older) living in the sample of municipalities that are part of the study group. . Smallest Geographic Unit: Municipality

items.name: Data and Code for: Experimental Evidence on the Effectiveness of Non-Experts for Improving Vaccine Demand
items.description: We experimentally vary signals and senders to identify which combination will increase vaccine demand among a disadvantaged population in the United States -- Black and White men without a college education. Our main finding is that laypeople (non-expert concordant senders) are most effective at promoting vaccination, particularly among those least willing to become vaccinated. This finding points to a trade-off between the higher qualifications of experts on the one hand, but lower social proximity to low socio-economic status populations on the other hand, which may undermine credibility in settings of low trust. Response Rates: N/A. Recruited through online survey panels that do not provide information on response rates. Recruited through various online survey panels (Qualtrics, Lucid, CloudResearch), as well as Facebook advertisements. Aimed for a sample with an age distribution representative of that of men aged 25-51 with no college education in the U.S. Non-college-educated Black and White men aged 25-51. . web-based survey;

items.name: Data and Code for: EARLY HOME VISITING DELIVERY MODEL AND MENTAL HEALTH
items.description: This study analyses the impacts of a prenatal and infancy home visiting program targeting disadvantaged families on mental health outcomes, assessed through diagnostic interviews. The program significantly reduced the prevalence of mental health conditions for both mothers and children, measured at primary school age, and broke the intergenerational association of these conditions. The impacts are predominantly associated with a particular delivery model, wherein a single home visitor interacts with the family, as opposed to a model involving two home visitors. Presence of Common Scales: Expert System for Diagnosis of Mental Disorders (DIA-X) and Diagnostic Interview forMental Disorders for Children and Adolescents (DIPS) Disadvantaged mothers and their children participating in the Pro Kind research trial . computer-assisted personal interview (CAPI); computer-assisted telephone interview (CATI); Data was collected at the home of the families and via telephone interviews

items.name: Data and Code for: All Eyes on Them: A Field Experiment on Citizen Oversight and Electoral Integrity
items.description: Can Information and Communication Technologies help citizens monitor their elections? We analyze a large-scale field experiment designed to answer this question in Colombia. We leveraged Facebook advertisements sent to over 4 million potential voters to encourage citizen reporting of electoral irregularities. We also cross-randomized whether candidates were informed about the campaign in a subset of municipalities. Total reports, and evidence-backed ones, experienced a large increase. Across a wide array of measures, electoral irregularities decreased. Finally, the reporting campaign reduced the vote share of candidates dependent on irregularities. This light-touch intervention is more cost-effective than monitoring efforts traditionally used by policymakers. Study group intervention: 698 municipalities in Colombia. Surveys: adult Facebook users (aged 18 years or older) living in the sample of municipalities that are part of the study group. . Smallest Geographic Unit: Municipality
items.citationHtml: Garbiras-Díaz, Natalia; Montenegro, Mateo, 2026, "Data and Code for: All Eyes on Them: A Field Experiment on Citizen Oversight and Electoral Integrity", https://doi.org/10.3886/E160921, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Market Access and Quality Upgrading: Evidence from Four Experiments
items.description: Smallholder farming in many developing countries is characterized by low productivity and low quality output. Low quality limits the price farmers can command and their potential income. We conduct a series of experiments among maize farmers in Uganda to shed light on the barriers to quality upgrading and to study its potential. First, we document that quality is low but partly observable. Second, we show that the causal return to quality is zero, suggesting that the market for quality maize is effectively missing. Third, we generate experimental variation in access to a market for premium quality maize, combined with training on agricultural best-practices, and document large increases in both farm productivity and income. Fourth, we show that agricultural training alone does not affect agricultural outcomes. Our findings reveal the importance of demand-side constraints in limiting rural income and productivity growth. Smallholder households in Uganda.
items.citationHtml: Bold, Tessa; Ghisolfi, Selene; Nsonzi, Frances; Svensson, Jakob, 2026, "Data and Code for: Market Access and Quality Upgrading: Evidence from Four Experiments", https://doi.org/10.3886/E158401V1-156134, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Replication data for: The Effect of Cash, Vouchers, and Food Transfers on Intimate Partner Violence: Evidence from a Randomized Experiment in Northern Ecuador
items.description: Using a randomized experiment in Ecuador, this study provides evidence on whether cash, vouchers, and food transfers targeted to women and intended to reduce poverty and food insecurity also affected intimate partner violence. Results indicate that transfers reduce controlling behaviors and physical and/or sexual violence by 6 to 7 percentage points. Impacts do not vary by transfer modality, which provides evidence that transfers not only have the potential to decrease violence in the short-term, but also that cash is just as effective as in-kind transfers. The program was implemented in seven urban centers with large Colombian refugee populations in the provinces of Carchi and Sucumbios in Northern Ecuador. To determine program qualification, all households within the pre-chosen neighborhoods were mapped and administered a short census survey. Households were ranked according to a proxy means test and a cut off score to determine program eligibility was implemented based on project budget constraints. Random sample of beneficiaries are included in the dataset.
items.citationHtml: Hidrobo, Melissa; Peterman, Amber; Heise, Lori, 2026, "Replication data for: The Effect of Cash, Vouchers, and Food Transfers on Intimate Partner Violence: Evidence from a Randomized Experiment in Northern Ecuador", https://doi.org/10.3886/E113634V1-118220, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: ECIN Replication Package for "A Simple Nudge Increases Socioeconomic Diversity in Undergraduate Economics"
items.description: We assess whether a light-touch intervention can increase socioeconomic and racial diversity in undergraduate Economics. We randomly assigned over 2,200 students a message with basic information about the Economics major; the basic message combined with an emphasis on the rewarding careers or financial returns associated with the major; or no message. Messages increased the proportion of first generation students majoring in Economics by five percentage points. This effect size was sufficient to reverse the gap in Economics majors for first generation students. Suggestive evidence indicates the effects may be driven by first generation students who were not also underrepresented minorities. Response Rates: 100% (no attrition from administrative data) all students in eligible classes who consented to participate in study Oregon State University students enrolled in Economics Principles courses on Corvallis campus. record abstracts; web-based survey;
items.citationHtml: Pugatch, Todd; Schroeder, Elizabeth, 2026, "ECIN Replication Package for "A Simple Nudge Increases Socioeconomic Diversity in Undergraduate Economics"", https://doi.org/10.3886/E192268V2, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Women Left Behind: Gender Disparities in Utilization of Government Health Insurance in India
items.description: This package contains the survey data and documentation for “Women Left Behind: Gender Disparities in Utilization of Government Health Insurance in India,” by Pascaline Dupas and Radhika Jain. It contains data collected in Rajasthan, India between the Summer of 2017 and the Fall of 2020, as well as the survey instruments used to collect it. The abstract of the paper is as follows: We document large gender disparities within a government program that entitles 46 million poor individuals to free hospital care. We show that care is not free in practice and higher costs are associated with larger disparities. Lowering care costs increases female utilization but does not reduce gender disparities because marginal beneficiaries are as likely to be male as inframarginals. Long-term exposure to local female leaders reduces disparities by addressing factors lowering female care. In the presence of gender bias, subsidizing social services may fail to address gender inequalities without actions that specifically target females. - 20,000 randomly selected participants of the Bhamashah Swasthya Bima Yojana program (Audit surveys) - 1,164 rural households that had a BSBY-covered childbirth in 2017 (HH survey) - 561 GP Sarpanches in November 2020 (Sarpanch survey).
items.citationHtml: Dupas, Pascaline; Jain, Radhika, 2026, "Data and Code for: Women Left Behind: Gender Disparities in Utilization of Government Health Insurance in India", https://doi.org/10.3886/E199081, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: Civil Liberties in Times of Crisis
items.description: Major crises --- from terrorist attacks to epidemic outbreaks --- bring the trade-off between individual civil liberties and societal well-being into sharp relief. In this paper, we study how willing citizens are to restrict civil liberties to improve public health conditions in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic. We design and conduct representative surveys involving approximately 550,000 responses across 15 countries, including China and the United States, during many months of the COVID-19 pandemic, from March 2020 until January 2021. We document significant heterogeneity across countries and demographic groups in willingness to sacrifice rights for public welfare. Citizens disadvantaged by income, education, or race are less willing to sacrifice rights than their more advantaged peers in every country, as are those with prior experience in communist regimes. Leveraging naturally occurring variation as well as experimental approaches, we estimate that a one standard deviation increase in health security concerns increases willingness to sacrifice civil liberties by approximately 68\%-83\% of the difference between the average Chinese and U.S. citizen. Stated preferences correlate with observed behavior including demand for tracing apps, donations, and petitions. Persons aged 18 and over living in the countries indicated under "Geographic Coverage" above. The in-depth data were collected through a survey platform "Qualtrics".
items.citationHtml: Alsan, Marcella; Braghieri, Luca; Eichmeyer, Sarah; Kim, Minjeong Joyce; Stantcheva, Stefanie; Yang, David Y., 2026, "Data and Code for: Civil Liberties in Times of Crisis", https://doi.org/10.3886/E176601V1, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Code for: SHOULD COLLEGE BE “FREE”? EVIDENCE ON FREE COLLEGE, EARLY COMMITMENT, AND MERIT AID FROM AN EIGHT-YEAR RANDOMIZED TRIAL
items.description: We provide evidence on the effects of college financial aid from an eight-year randomized trial offering high school ninth graders a $12,000 merit-based grant. The program was designed to be free of tuition and fees at community colleges and substantially lower the cost of attending four-year colleges. During high school, eligibility for the grant increased students’ expectations of college attendance and low-cost college preparation effort, but not higher-cost effort. The program likely increased graduation from two-year colleges, perhaps because it was framed as making two-year college free, but did not affect overall college entry, graduation, employment, incarceration, or teen pregnancy. Additional analysis helps explain these modest effects and variation in results across prior studies. Since there was no sampling design, there are no sampling weights. We did not apply non-response weights because missingness was generally balanced. Response Rates: Most of the measures were not survey items. The information in this section pertains only to the high school exit survey administered by the school district. The response rate for the high school exit survey was 53 percent. See additional details (e.g. item response) in the paper. Presence of Common Scales: Not applicable. The district surveyed all MPS 12th graders. All 9th graders in Milwaukee Public Schools in the fall of 2011. Smallest Geographic Unit: Not applicable. on-site questionnaire;

items.name: Data and Code for: Noise, Cognitive Function, and Worker Productivity
items.description: This is the data and code needed to replicate the results in Noise, Cognitive Function, and Worker Productivity. Paper Abstract: Cognitive science research suggests the noisy workplaces common in low and middle income countries can impair workers’ cognitive functions. However, whether this translates into lower earnings for workers depends on the importance of these functions for productivity and whether workers understand these effects. I use two randomized experiments in Nairobi, Kenya to answer these questions. First, I randomize exposure to engine noise during a textile training course at a government training facility. An increase of 7 dB reduces productivity by approximately 3%. In order to study what mechanism drives this effect, I then randomize engine noise during tests of cognitive function and an effort task. The same noise change impairs cognitive function but not effort task performance. Finally, in both experiments, I examine whether individuals appreciate the impact of noise on their performance by eliciting participants’ willingness to pay for quiet working conditions while randomly varying whether they are compensated based on their performance. Individuals’ willingness to pay does not depend on the wage structure; suggesting that they are not aware that quiet working conditions would increase their performance pay. Thus, workers may fail to mitigate earnings losses by sorting into quieter jobs where they are more productive. Individuals seeking work at textile factories in Athi River Textile workers in Nairobi, Kenya. Smallest Geographic Unit: Individuals cognitive assessment test; face-to-face interview;
items.citationHtml: Dean, Joshua T, 2026, "Data and Code for: Noise, Cognitive Function, and Worker Productivity", https://doi.org/10.3886/E193705V1-175061, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: The Impact of COVID-19 on Access to Mental Healthcare Services
items.description: The COVID-19 pandemic increased the rate of mental health disorders, as well as demand for mental health services. It remains unclear, however, the extent to which the pandemic impacted access to mental health services. Using data from an audit field experiment, we examine the impact of COVID-19 on access to mental health care appointments in the United States. This experiment ran from January to May 2020 and overlapped with the initial onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Response Rates: We sent 1 message to 1000 mental health practitioners.We received 755 response messages. In order to be included in our sample, a mental health practitioner (MHP) must: 1. must not specialize exclusively on specific types of patients who are outside of the scope of our experiment (e.g., children, or couples therapy; 2. must not be specialized in a type of therapy (e.g., grief, domestic violence) that would not deal with the common mental health condition; 3. must list an individual's profile (e.g., it cannot be the profile of a clinic); 4. must provide an option to email them through a web form; 5. must be accepting patients (i.e., we do not contact MHPs that indicate that they are not currently accepting patients). After accounting for these characteristics, we select MHPs proportionately to state populations; specifically, we divide states in terciles, based on their population. Within states, we select MHPs proportionally to the population of each ZIP code such that our final sample is nationally representative. Mental health practitioners across the US . Smallest Geographic Unit: State other;
items.citationHtml: Harrell, Benjamin, 2026, "Data and Code for: The Impact of COVID-19 on Access to Mental Healthcare Services", https://doi.org/10.3886/E183121, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

items.name: Data and Code for: The impact of improving access to support services for victims of domestic violence on demand for services and victim outcomes
items.description: These are the data and replication files to accompany our study "The impact of improving access to support services for victims of domestic violence on demand for services and victim outcomes". In this study, we conducted a randomised controlled trial of an intervention designed to assist victims of domestic violence in accessing non-police support services. The intervention led to a 22% decrease in the fraction of victims providing a witness statement to police. Witness statements are an important piece of evidence and a key input in the prosecution of perpetrators. Despite this, we do not nd a signicant change in perpetrator arrests and convictions or in reported future violence. Survey responses provide evidence of an increase in non-police service use, a reduction in future victimisation risk, but also a potential decrease in short-run well-being. Response Rates: The data is composed of two parts. The administrative data reflects the universe of cases which meet the study's criteria. The survey was administered to a 25% sample of cases. The response rate is 84%. From the cases in the subject pool 25% were randomly sampled for surveying. These data reflect all police-reported cases of domestic violence in the county of Leicestershire, UK, between November 2014 and April 2015, for which: (1) the report was filed as a domestic incident; (2) in the previous 365 days, the victim had shown up in at least three and fewerthan seven DV reports (including the current one); (3) the victim was not previously in the subject pool (in either the treatment or control group); (4) responding officers did not recommend the victim for a pre-existing intervention. . Smallest Geographic Unit: County of Leicestershire, UK.

items.name: Data and code for "The Role of Behavioral Frictions in Health Insurance Marketplace Enrollment and Risk: Evidence from a Field Experiment"
items.description: We experimentally varied information mailed to 87,000 households in California's health insurance marketplace to study the role of frictions in insurance take-up. Reminders about the enrollment deadline raised enrollment by 1.3 pp (16 percent) in this typically low take-up population. Heterogeneous effects of personalized subsidy information indicate misperceptions about program benefits. Consistent with an adverse selection model with frictional enrollment costs, the intervention lowered average spending risk by 5.1 percent, implying that marginal respondents were 37 percent less costly than inframarginal consumers. We observe the largest positive selection among low income consumers, who exhibit the largest frictions in enrollment. Finally, we estimate the implied value of the letter intervention to be $25 to $53 per month in subsidy dollars. These results suggest that frictions may partially explain low take-up for marketplace insurance, and that interventions reducing them can improve enrollment and market risk in exchanges. The attached code and data replicates results reported in this study. Response Rates: Data are from administrative sources, so the data includes information on all RCT study subjects. We began with the set of households in the Funnel who were deemed eligible for the study at the time of treatment assignment. For budgetary reasons, we reduced the total sample to 126,182 randomly selected households from the full Funnel to be in the study. We then excluded individuals who did not have valid addresses, ages, or other eligibility attributes. The final sample size after applying these exclusions was 87,394 households. The universe of data come from subjects of the Open Enrollment RCT conducted in December 2015 through January 2016. The RCT sample was drawn from the population of people in the Covered California "Funnel," denoting people who had begun the enrollment process, or had been referred to Covered California from the county systems, but had not yet enrolled in a marketplace place. .
items.citationHtml: Domurat, Richard; Menashe, Isaac; Yin, Wesley, 2026, "Data and code for "The Role of Behavioral Frictions in Health Insurance Marketplace Enrollment and Risk: Evidence from a Field Experiment"", https://doi.org/10.3886/E125801, ICPSR - Interuniversity Consortium for Political and Social Research

Smallest Geographic Unit: County of Leicestershire, UK.
